name: Samsung Ballie
short_description: A bowling-ball-sized rolling home robot with a built-in projector and AI assistant that autonomously navigates the house and manages connected appliances.
long_description: Ballie is a spherical, self-driving domestic robot that uses a spatial LiDAR sensor to roam the home and a dual-lens 1080p projector to display video calls, movies, workout guides, and greetings onto walls and floors. It connects to and orchestrates smart-home devices, monitors pets and family, and learns user routines to deliver personalized help. First shown as a concept at CES 2020 and revamped at CES 2024, Ballie matters as a mainstream consumer attempt to make an ambient, mobile AI a cohabiting presence in everyday domestic life rather than a fixed appliance.

images.caption: Samsung Ballie, the yellow spherical AI companion robot, beside its projector on a table at CES 2024.
images.alt_text: A small yellow spherical robot sits on a wooden table next to a projector unit in a home setting with a bookshelf in the background.

sources.title: A Day in the Life With Ballie: An AI Companion Robot for the Home
sources.access_notes: Official Samsung Newsroom announcement of the CES 2024 Ballie.

sources.title: Samsung brings back Ballie, its home robot, at CES 2024 — with a few upgrades
sources.access_notes: Reports bowling-ball size, spatial LiDAR, dual-lens 1080p projector, 2-3 hour battery.

interactions.title: Lie on the floor and the movie follows you up to the ceiling
interactions.description: Ballie detects your posture and facial angle and repositions its dual-lens projector accordingly — casting a workout video onto the wall while you stand, then onto the ceiling once you lie down on the mat.

interactions.title: Calms the dog while you are out
interactions.description: Spotting the dog getting into mischief on its patrol, Ballie rolls over, triggers a connected feeder to dispense food, and projects a moving video onto the floor for the pet to chase — then sends you a clip of it on your phone.

interactions.title: Blasts the old window AC with an infrared zap
interactions.description: Beyond SmartThings gadgets, Ballie carries an infrared transmitter, so it can roll up and switch on "dumb" devices like an older air conditioner or a pre-smart TV the way a remote would.

interactions.title: "Hey Ballie, how do I look?"
interactions.description: Standing in front of it while getting dressed, you ask Ballie how your outfit reads; using its camera and the onboard Gemini model, it offers styling suggestions for the day ahead.

interactions.title: The companion that never quite moves in
interactions.description: First teased in 2020 and re-demoed every CES since, Ballie has been repeatedly delayed and, by 2026, indefinitely shelved — reporters note Samsung itself struggled to say what a $2,000 rolling robot is for when homes already have speakers, tablets, and a vacuum.

interactions.title: Projects the recipe onto the counter, not a phone you have to wipe
interactions.description: Because it can already aim a focused image onto any nearby surface and tracks where you are standing, Ballie could throw the next step of a recipe onto the kitchen counter beside you so messy hands never touch a screen.

interactions.title: Walks a guest to the spare room using its floor map
interactions.description: Ballie builds a LiDAR floor plan of the home and knows where things are; a visitor could follow the rolling bot down the hall as it projects a soft arrow on the floor and leads them to the guest bedroom.
